content.text: When something goes wrong in a traditional web service, rolling back is mostly a matter of deploying the previous container image and waiting for health checks to pass. When something goes wrong in an agentic AI service, rollback is simultaneously harder and higher-stakes: you may be reverting not just code but a language model version, a system prompt, a set of tool schemas, a vector index, and the state of dozens of in-flight multi-turn agent sessions that customers are actively using. A rollback runbook for an agent service is the documented, pre-rehearsed answer to the question: exactly what do we do, in what order, with what commands, verified by what checks?
What It Is

A rollback runbook for an agentic AI service is a written, version-controlled, executable playbook that describes every discrete action an on-call engineer must take to return the system to a known-good state after a bad deployment, a prompt regression, a model upgrade gone wrong, or any other class of incident that requires reverting to a prior version. It differs from a general incident response guide in two important ways.
First, it is
component-specific

. An agentic service is not a single artifact — it is a composition of application code, model endpoint configuration, system prompts, retrieval indices, tool definitions, feature flags, and agent state stores. Each component has its own versioning scheme and its own rollback mechanism. The runbook must address each component in sequence and in parallel where possible.
Second, it accounts for
stateful, in-flight sessions

. Traditional stateless services can be cut over instantly. Agent services accumulate multi-turn conversation state, partial task execution, and tool call results that belong to real users in the middle of real workflows. The runbook must specify how to handle these sessions — drain them, snapshot them, or immediately terminate them — before the system version changes underneath them.
Why It Matters
Agentic AI services compound risk in ways that make having a precise runbook more important than it is for conventional services.

Model regressions are non-obvious. A bad code deployment typically produces an immediate spike in 5xx errors. A bad model upgrade may produce subtly worse outputs — hallucinated tool calls, misinterpreted instructions, degraded memory recall — that only become visible when users report problems hours or days later. By the time you decide to roll back, the bad state may have propagated through conversation histories, downstream systems, and stored agent memories.

Prompt changes are invisible to most deployment pipelines. System prompts are rarely compiled artifacts. They often live as strings in configuration files, environment variables, or databases. They may not be versioned alongside application code. When a prompt regression occurs, an on-call engineer without a runbook may not even know which artifact to revert.

Long-running agents are difficult to interrupt safely. A user may be twenty turns into a research session or a code-generation task. Killing the session mid-task without notifying the user or preserving their progress is a significant user experience failure. The runbook must provide a decision tree for how to handle in-flight sessions, not just how to flip the infrastructure switches.

The mean time to restore (MTTR) for AI services is typically longer than for web services because the rollback surface is larger. A runbook reduces MTTR by eliminating the cognitive overhead of figuring out what to do under stress. Engineers who have drilled on a runbook execute it faster and with fewer errors than engineers who are improvising.
Benefits
Reduced MTTR
. Pre-written commands and decision trees eliminate the minutes spent figuring out what to do. Verified runbooks routinely cut MTTR in half compared to improvised responses.
Reduced blast radius
. Explicit pre-rollback snapshot steps mean you have a record of the state you are reverting from, which limits scope creep and makes post-incident analysis possible.
Safer session handling
. Explicit drain and snapshot steps prevent users from losing work and prevent the agent from applying the reverted model to a conversation context that was built against a different model.
On-call rotation safety
. A new on-call engineer who has never seen the system before can execute a well-written runbook at 3 AM without being an expert in every subsystem.
Audit trail
. Executed runbooks become the canonical record of what was done, making post-mortems faster and more accurate.
Trade-offs
Runbook rot

. A runbook written once and never updated becomes actively dangerous. Commands change, namespaces change, model version identifiers change. A stale runbook gives engineers false confidence and may execute the wrong action.
Over-specification creates fragility

. A runbook that specifies every flag of every command may break the moment a CLI is upgraded. The right balance is specificity of intent with flexibility on exact syntax, supplemented by verification steps that catch mismatches.
Session drain delays rollback

. Waiting for in-flight sessions to complete naturally can delay the rollback by minutes. The runbook must set an explicit time-box — typically 5–10 minutes — after which sessions are forcibly terminated.
Model version pinning may not be available

. Some model providers do not offer stable versioned endpoints. If your provider retires model versions without notice, the runbook's model rollback step may fail. This is a dependency risk to document and mitigate during design.
How It Works

A rollback runbook for an agentic AI service is structured as a linear sequence of phases, each with explicit entry criteria, actions, and verification steps. The phases are: trigger, pre-rollback snapshot, session drain, component rollback, verification, and communication. Within the component rollback phase, multiple components are rolled back in parallel by different team members or by an orchestration script.

The runbook is triggered either by an automated alert (e.g., error rate exceeds 5% for three consecutive minutes) or by a manual decision from the on-call engineer or incident commander. The trigger sets the incident clock: all subsequent times are measured from trigger time (T+0).

Each step in the runbook has three parts: the action to take, the exact command or click path, and the verification criterion that confirms the action succeeded. Engineers mark each step complete before proceeding to the next. This eliminates the ambiguity of "I think I did that" during a stressful incident.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the five-phase flow of a rollback runbook for an agentic AI service, the five component tracks that are executed in parallel during the rollback phase, and the post-rollback verification checklist.

attrs.alt: Rollback Runbook Flow for Agentic AI Services — five phases (trigger, snapshot, drain, rollback, verify) with five parallel component tracks and post-rollback verification checklist

content.text: When to Use It
A rollback runbook should be executed — in whole or in part — in any of the following situations:

A deployment of new application code, model version, or system prompt causes a measurable degradation in error rate, latency, or user-reported quality within the rollback window (typically 30–60 minutes after deploy).
Automated canary or shadow evaluation detects a statistically significant drop in LLM output quality score compared to the previous version.
A safety or compliance violation is detected in agent outputs — for example, the agent begins generating disallowed content, calling unauthorized tools, or leaking data across user contexts.
A go/no-go decision is made to roll back a partial rollout before it progresses to the next ring.
The model provider announces a breaking change to a model version that your service depends on, and you need to pin to the previous stable version while you assess compatibility.

Not every rollback requires executing all five phases. A prompt-only regression may only require rolling back the system prompt and feature flags, leaving application code and infrastructure untouched. The runbook should have clearly marked optional phases so engineers can execute the minimal necessary subset.
How to Implement

The following is a complete rollback runbook script. It is designed to be run by a single on-call engineer and produces a structured log of every action taken, with timestamps, for use in the post-incident review.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# rollback.sh — Agentic AI Service Rollback Runbook
# Usage: ./rollback.sh [--reason "<incident description>"] [--skip-drain] [--skip-app]
# Requires: kubectl, aws cli, curl, jq
set -euo pipefail

INCIDENT_ID="INC-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
LOG_FILE="/var/log/rollback/${INCIDENT_ID}.log"
mkdir -p /var/log/rollback
exec > >(tee -a "$LOG_FILE") 2>&1

RED='\033[0;31m'; GREEN='\033[0;32m'; YELLOW='\033[1;33m'; NC='\033[0m'
step() { echo -e "\n${YELLOW}[$(date '+%H:%M:%S')] STEP $1: $2${NC}"; }
ok()   { echo -e "${GREEN}  OK: $1${NC}"; }
fail() { echo -e "${RED}  FAIL: $1${NC}"; exit 1; }

# ── CONFIGURATION ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
NAMESPACE="agent-prod"
DEPLOYMENT="agent-service"
PREVIOUS_IMAGE_TAG="${PREVIOUS_IMAGE_TAG:-}"    # must be set or auto-detected
PREVIOUS_MODEL_VERSION="${PREV_MODEL:-claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022}"
PREVIOUS_PROMPT_VERSION="${PREV_PROMPT:-v47}"   # git tag or S3 key
FEATURE_FLAG_SERVICE="https://flags.internal/api"
DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=300
SKIP_DRAIN="${SKIP_DRAIN:-false}"

# ── PHASE 1: TRIGGER & ANNOUNCEMENT ──────────────────────────────────────────
step 1 "Record trigger and announce incident"
echo "Incident ID: $INCIDENT_ID"
echo "Triggered at: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
echo "Reason: ${1:-manual}"
# Notify Slack incident channel
curl -s -X POST "$SLACK_WEBHOOK" \
  -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
  --data "{\"text\":\"ROLLBACK INITIATED: $INCIDENT_ID — ${1:-manual}. On-call: $USER. Runbook: $LOG_FILE\"}" \
  && ok "Slack notification sent" || echo "WARNING: Slack notification failed"

# ── PHASE 2: PRE-ROLLBACK SNAPSHOT ───────────────────────────────────────────
step 2 "Capture current state before rolling back"
SNAPSHOT_DIR="/var/log/rollback/${INCIDENT_ID}-snapshot"
mkdir -p "$SNAPSHOT_DIR"

# Capture current deployment image
kubectl get deployment "$DEPLOYMENT" -n "$NAMESPACE" -o json \
  > "$SNAPSHOT_DIR/deployment.json"
CURRENT_IMAGE=$(jq -r '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image' \
  "$SNAPSHOT_DIR/deployment.json")
echo "Current image: $CURRENT_IMAGE" | tee "$SNAPSHOT_DIR/current-image.txt"

# Capture current config map (contains model version and prompt pointer)
kubectl get configmap agent-config -n "$NAMESPACE" -o yaml \
  > "$SNAPSHOT_DIR/agent-config.yaml"

# Capture current feature flag state
curl -s "$FEATURE_FLAG_SERVICE/dump" \
  > "$SNAPSHOT_DIR/feature-flags.json"

# Capture active session count
ACTIVE_SESSIONS=$(curl -s https://agent.internal/admin/sessions/count | jq .count)
echo "Active sessions at rollback start: $ACTIVE_SESSIONS"

ok "Snapshot written to $SNAPSHOT_DIR"

# ── PHASE 3: DRAIN IN-FLIGHT SESSIONS ────────────────────────────────────────
step 3 "Drain or cut in-flight agent sessions"
if [[ "$SKIP_DRAIN" == "true" ]]; then
  echo "Drain skipped by flag (immediate cut)"
  curl -s -X POST https://agent.internal/admin/sessions/terminate-all \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN"
else
  echo "Stopping new session creation (maintenance mode ON)"
  curl -s -X PUT "$FEATURE_FLAG_SERVICE/flags/accept-new-sessions" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $FLAG_TOKEN" \
    -d '{"enabled": false}'

  echo "Waiting up to ${DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS}s for sessions to drain..."
  DEADLINE=$((SECONDS + DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS))
  while [[ $SECONDS -lt $DEADLINE ]]; do
    REMAINING=$(curl -s https://agent.internal/admin/sessions/count | jq .count)
    echo "  Active sessions: $REMAINING"
    [[ "$REMAINING" -eq 0 ]] && break
    sleep 10
  done

  REMAINING=$(curl -s https://agent.internal/admin/sessions/count | jq .count)
  if [[ "$REMAINING" -gt 0 ]]; then
    echo "Drain timeout reached with $REMAINING sessions still active. Force-terminating."
    curl -s -X POST https://agent.internal/admin/sessions/terminate-all \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN"
  fi
fi
ok "Sessions drained"

# ── PHASE 4a: ROLL BACK APPLICATION CODE ─────────────────────────────────────
step 4a "Roll back application deployment"
if [[ -n "$PREVIOUS_IMAGE_TAG" ]]; then
  kubectl set image deployment/"$DEPLOYMENT" \
    agent="agent-service:${PREVIOUS_IMAGE_TAG}" -n "$NAMESPACE"
else
  echo "No PREVIOUS_IMAGE_TAG set — using kubectl rollout undo"
  kubectl rollout undo deployment/"$DEPLOYMENT" -n "$NAMESPACE"
fi
kubectl rollout status deployment/"$DEPLOYMENT" -n "$NAMESPACE" --timeout=120s \
  && ok "Deployment rollback complete" || fail "Deployment rollback failed"

# ── PHASE 4b: ROLL BACK MODEL VERSION ────────────────────────────────────────
step 4b "Roll back model version in ConfigMap"
kubectl patch configmap agent-config -n "$NAMESPACE" \
  --type merge \
  -p "{\"data\": {\"MODEL_VERSION\": \"$PREVIOUS_MODEL_VERSION\"}}"

# Restart pods to pick up new configmap
kubectl rollout restart deployment/"$DEPLOYMENT" -n "$NAMESPACE"
kubectl rollout status deployment/"$DEPLOYMENT" -n "$NAMESPACE" --timeout=120s \
  && ok "Model version rolled back to $PREVIOUS_MODEL_VERSION" \
  || fail "Pod restart after model version change failed"

# ── PHASE 4c: ROLL BACK SYSTEM PROMPT ────────────────────────────────────────
step 4c "Roll back system prompt to version $PREVIOUS_PROMPT_VERSION"
# Fetch previous prompt from S3 (or git — substitute your prompt store)
aws s3 cp \
  "s3://agent-prompts/system-prompt-${PREVIOUS_PROMPT_VERSION}.txt" \
  /tmp/system-prompt.txt

# Update the prompt via the admin API (agent service reloads on PUT)
curl -s -X PUT https://agent.internal/admin/config/system-prompt \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: text/plain" \
  --data-binary @/tmp/system-prompt.txt \
  && ok "System prompt reverted to $PREVIOUS_PROMPT_VERSION" \
  || fail "System prompt update failed"

# ── PHASE 4d: ROLL BACK FEATURE FLAGS ────────────────────────────────────────
step 4d "Disable new-version feature flags"
for flag in "enable-new-tool-schema" "enable-model-v2" "enable-streaming-memory"; do
  curl -s -X PUT "$FEATURE_FLAG_SERVICE/flags/$flag" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $FLAG_TOKEN" \
    -d '{"enabled": false}' \
    && echo "  Disabled: $flag" || echo "  WARNING: failed to disable $flag"
done
# Re-enable session acceptance
curl -s -X PUT "$FEATURE_FLAG_SERVICE/flags/accept-new-sessions" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $FLAG_TOKEN" \
  -d '{"enabled": true}'
ok "Feature flags reverted"

# ── PHASE 4e: ROLL BACK TOOL CONFIGURATIONS ──────────────────────────────────
step 4e "Restore previous tool schema version"
aws s3 cp \
  "s3://agent-tool-configs/tool-schema-${PREVIOUS_PROMPT_VERSION}.json" \
  /tmp/tool-schema.json
curl -s -X PUT https://agent.internal/admin/config/tool-schema \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  --data-binary @/tmp/tool-schema.json \
  && ok "Tool schema reverted" || fail "Tool schema update failed"

# ── PHASE 5: VERIFICATION ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
step 5 "Verify rollback succeeded"
# Check all pods running
READY=$(kubectl get deployment "$DEPLOYMENT" -n "$NAMESPACE" \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}')
DESIRED=$(kubectl get deployment "$DEPLOYMENT" -n "$NAMESPACE" \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}')
[[ "$READY" == "$DESIRED" ]] \
  && ok "Pods: $READY/$DESIRED ready" \
  || fail "Pods not fully ready: $READY/$DESIRED"

# Check health endpoint
HTTP_STATUS=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
  https://agent.internal/health)
[[ "$HTTP_STATUS" == "200" ]] \
  && ok "Health endpoint: 200" \
  || fail "Health endpoint returned $HTTP_STATUS"

# Confirm model version
ACTUAL_MODEL=$(curl -s https://agent.internal/admin/config | jq -r .model_version)
[[ "$ACTUAL_MODEL" == "$PREVIOUS_MODEL_VERSION" ]] \
  && ok "Model version confirmed: $ACTUAL_MODEL" \
  || fail "Model version mismatch: expected $PREVIOUS_MODEL_VERSION, got $ACTUAL_MODEL"

# Smoke test: send a known-good agent prompt and check response
RESPONSE=$(curl -s -X POST https://agent.internal/v1/chat \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SMOKE_TEST_TOKEN" \
  -d '{"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2?"}]}')
ECHO=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.choices[0].message.content // empty')
[[ -n "$ECHO" ]] \
  && ok "Smoke test passed — agent responded" \
  || fail "Smoke test failed — no response content"

# ── FINAL NOTIFICATION ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
curl -s -X POST "$SLACK_WEBHOOK" \
  -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
  --data "{\"text\":\"ROLLBACK COMPLETE: $INCIDENT_ID. All checks passed. Model: $PREVIOUS_MODEL_VERSION. Log: $LOG_FILE\"}"

echo -e "\n${GREEN}Rollback $INCIDENT_ID completed at $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)${NC}"
echo "Full log: $LOG_FILE"
Examples
Model version rollback
. Your agent was using
claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022

and you upgraded it to a new snapshot version. Automated quality evaluation detected a 12% drop in task completion rate. You need to pin back to the previous version. The model rollback step patches the ConfigMap and restarts pods:

# Pin model version in ConfigMap
kubectl patch configmap agent-config -n agent-prod \
  --type merge \
  -p '{"data": {"MODEL_VERSION": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"}}'

# Rolling restart to pick up new configmap
kubectl rollout restart deployment/agent-service -n agent-prod
kubectl rollout status deployment/agent-service -n agent-prod --timeout=120s

# Verify the running pods use the right model version
kubectl exec -it \
  $(kubectl get pod -n agent-prod -l app=agent-service -o name | head -1) \
  -- env | grep MODEL_VERSION
System prompt rollback
. A new system prompt was deployed that inadvertently removed a safety instruction. You need to revert to the previous prompt version (v47) stored in S3:

# Fetch the previous prompt version
aws s3 cp s3://agent-prompts/system-prompt-v47.txt /tmp/system-prompt-v47.txt
cat /tmp/system-prompt-v47.txt  # verify content before applying

# Push it to the live service
curl -X PUT https://agent.internal/admin/config/system-prompt \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: text/plain" \
  --data-binary @/tmp/system-prompt-v47.txt

# Confirm the service reports the expected prompt hash
curl -s https://agent.internal/admin/config | jq .system_prompt_version
Real-World Example

An enterprise software company deployed a new version of their customer support agent that included a revised system prompt intended to make responses more concise. The prompt change accidentally removed a line that restricted the agent to only discuss their own product and prevented it from disparaging competitors. Within 40 minutes of the deploy, the first user reports arrived: the agent had begun making unflattering comparisons to competitor products.

The on-call engineer executed the rollback runbook. The pre-rollback snapshot captured the bad prompt text and the current active session count (37 sessions). The engineer chose graceful drain rather than immediate cut because the sessions were customer support interactions — abruptly terminating them would have made the incident worse from a customer experience perspective. After 4 minutes, 35 of the 37 sessions had naturally concluded. The 2 remaining sessions were older than 20 minutes and were force-terminated with an automated message to the customer: "Your session has been reset due to a service update. Your conversation history has been preserved."

The prompt rollback executed in under 2 minutes. The previous prompt was pulled from S3 (version v47, tagged at the prior deployment), pushed to the admin API, and the service immediately began using it for all new sessions. The verification step confirmed the correct prompt hash was active. Total elapsed time from trigger to resolution: 11 minutes. Without the runbook, the on-call engineer estimated the same recovery would have taken 30–45 minutes to piece together which artifact to revert and how to deploy it.
Common Mistakes
Not versioning system prompts alongside code.

If the system prompt is stored in a database or environment variable that is not tagged with the deployment version, there is no clean artifact to revert to. Always tag prompt deployments with a version identifier and store previous versions in a durable, retrievable location (S3, a versioned config store, or a Git-backed CMS).
Rolling back code without rolling back the model version.

Application code and model version can diverge. If a previous version of the code was designed for a previous model version, running old code against a new model may produce different behavior than the original deployment. The rollback must be atomic across both components.
Forgetting to disable feature flags.

If the bad behavior was enabled by a feature flag, rolling back the code without disabling the flag may leave the flag active, which means the old code will re-enable the problematic behavior if the flag is evaluated at runtime.
Cutting sessions without notifying users.

Abruptly terminating a multi-turn conversation without an explanation or session recovery message is a serious user experience failure. Always send a user-facing message when a session is force-terminated.
Not verifying the rollback succeeded.

Engineers under stress sometimes declare an incident resolved after executing the rollback commands without running the verification steps. The verification checklist exists precisely to catch cases where the rollback completed but the system is still not healthy — for example, because a cached value of the bad configuration is still being served.
Stale runbooks.

A runbook that references old Kubernetes namespace names, retired S3 bucket paths, or deprecated CLI flags is worse than no runbook because it gives false confidence. Runbooks must be updated as part of every deployment pipeline change and reviewed quarterly.
Best Practices
Co-locate the runbook with the deployment artifact.

Store the rollback runbook in the same repository as the application code, tagged alongside each release. This guarantees the runbook you execute corresponds exactly to the deployed version you are reverting.
Encode the previous version as a deployment metadata annotation.

Store PREVIOUS_IMAGE_TAG, PREVIOUS_MODEL_VERSION, and PREVIOUS_PROMPT_VERSION as Kubernetes annotations on the Deployment object at deploy time. This makes the rollback script self-contained — it reads the previous version from the object rather than requiring the engineer to know it.
Set an explicit drain time-box.

Never wait indefinitely for sessions to drain. Set a time-box (5–10 minutes is standard) and force-terminate after it expires. Document this time-box in the runbook and set user expectations accordingly.
Separate rollback concerns by component.

Design the runbook so that each component (code, model, prompt, flags, tools) can be rolled back independently. Not every incident requires a full rollback of all components; a partial rollback that targets only the changed component is faster and less disruptive.
Test the runbook in staging on every release.

The CI/CD pipeline should include a step that deploys the new version to staging and then executes the rollback script in dry-run mode. This catches broken runbook steps before they are needed in production.
Write runbook steps as verifiable assertions, not instructions.
Instead of "Restart the deployment," write "Restart the deployment and confirm all pods reach Ready state within 120 seconds." The verification criterion is as important as the action.
Performance and Scalability
Rollback duration is a key performance metric for AI services. The factors that drive rollback duration are different from traditional services.
Session drain time

is typically the bottleneck. If your agent service supports long-running sessions with 60-turn conversations, the natural drain time can be 15–20 minutes. The mitigation is to monitor active session age and set the time-box accordingly — a service with consistently long sessions should force-terminate earlier with a larger compensation payment (e.g., extended subscription credits) than one with short sessions.
Model endpoint cold start

affects rollback recovery time if you are switching model provider endpoints. Cold start times for some hosted model endpoints can be 20–30 seconds. Pre-warm the previous model endpoint if possible, or maintain a warm standby connection to it at all times.

At high scale (>10k active sessions), session state management becomes expensive. If session state is stored in a central Redis cluster, force-terminating thousands of sessions simultaneously can cause a write storm. The rollback script should include a rate-limited termination path that spreads session terminations over 30–60 seconds.
Tooling and Ecosystem
The following table maps each rollback component to the typical tooling used in production AI service deployments.
Component
Typical Tooling
Rollback Mechanism
Application code
Kubernetes, Helm, Argo CD
kubectl rollout undo or Helm rollback to previous release
Model version
Anthropic API, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock
ConfigMap patch to pin model identifier; provider-specific versioned endpoint
System prompt
S3 versioned bucket, Git-backed config store
Fetch previous version by tag; push to admin reload endpoint
Feature flags
LaunchDarkly, Unleash, AWS AppConfig
API call or dashboard toggle to disable new-version flags
Tool schemas
Custom config API, S3, database
Fetch and push previous schema version; trigger hot reload
Agent state / memory
PostgreSQL, Redis, vector store (Pinecone, Weaviate)
Point-in-time restore; tombstone entries written during bad version
Comparison with Alternatives

A rollback runbook is one of several incident recovery strategies. Understanding where it fits relative to the alternatives helps teams decide when to invest in a full rollback versus a faster mitigation.
Feature flag disable

: The fastest mitigation, taking seconds. Effective only if the bad behavior is entirely controlled by a flag. Cannot address infrastructure-level or model-level regressions. Should be the first mitigation attempted before a full rollback.
Traffic diversion

: Route traffic away from the bad version to an existing stable version running in another cluster or region. Effective for multi-region deployments but requires pre-provisioned capacity in the stable region. Faster than a full rollback because infrastructure changes are not needed.
Hot-patch deployment

: Deploy a targeted fix forward rather than reverting to the previous version. Preferred when the rollback would also revert unrelated improvements, or when the previous version itself had a known issue. Requires the engineering team to write and test the patch under incident conditions — high risk.
Full rollback runbook

: The safest and most comprehensive option when the cause of the regression is unclear or spans multiple components. Slower than the alternatives but returns the system to a fully verified known-good state.

The decision tree is: try feature flag disable first (30 seconds), then traffic diversion if available (2–5 minutes), then full rollback runbook (10–20 minutes). Never skip directly to a hot-patch deployment during an active incident unless the other options are not applicable.
Security and Reliability

Rollback runbooks introduce their own security surface. The admin APIs and credentials used to execute a rollback are high-privilege and must be protected with the same rigor as production deployment credentials.
Credential management for rollback

. The rollback script requires bearer tokens for the admin API, feature flag service, and model configuration endpoint. These credentials must not be hard-coded in the script. Use a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault) with an IAM role that can be assumed by the on-call engineer's principal. The credentials should have the minimum permissions necessary to execute the rollback and should be rotated after each incident.
Audit logging for rollback actions

. Every action taken during a rollback must be logged with the identity of the executor, the timestamp, and the exact command or API call. This is required for compliance audits and for post-incident analysis. The rollback script above achieves this by using
tee
to write all output to a named log file with the incident ID.
Integrity of the rollback artifact

. The previous-version image tag and prompt stored in S3 must be protected against tampering. Use S3 versioning and object lock to prevent overwriting of past versions. Sign container images with Cosign and verify the signature before rolling back to it. This prevents a scenario where an attacker who has compromised the artifact store injects a malicious image into the rollback path.
Two-person integrity for critical rollbacks

. For services handling sensitive data, require a second on-call engineer to confirm the rollback before execution. Implement this with a simple approval gate: the rollback script posts to Slack requesting approval, and a named approver responds with a thumbs-up emoji that the script polls for before proceeding.
Mental Model
Think of a rollback runbook as a
time machine with a checklist

. A traditional web service rollback is a time machine for the application binary. An agentic AI service rollback is a time machine for five different artifacts simultaneously: the application, the model, the prompt, the tool configuration, and the session state store. Each artifact has its own timeline, its own versioning scheme, and its own rollback mechanism.

The runbook is the checklist that coordinates those five time machines so they all land at the same point in the past — not one in the past and four in the present, which is often worse than no rollback at all. A model reverted to v1 running against a system prompt written for v2, with session state written by v2, is a Frankenstein deployment that can produce unpredictable behavior.
A secondary mental model: think of each phase of the runbook as a
gate with an assertion

. You do not move from phase N to phase N+1 until you have verified that phase N produced the expected outcome. This is the discipline that prevents "the rollback that made things worse" — the scenario where an engineer who moved too fast through the steps misconfigured one component and then moved on before catching it.
Key Takeaways
Post-mortem template
. After every executed rollback, complete the following blameless post-mortem within 48 hours.
POST-MORTEM TEMPLATE — Agentic AI Service Rollback
Date / time of trigger: _______________
Date / time of resolution: _______________
Total MTTR: _______________ minutes
Components rolled back (check all that apply): [ ] Application code [ ] Model version [ ] System prompt [ ] Feature flags [ ] Tool schemas [ ] Agent state
What triggered the rollback? (alert name, metric, user report)
What was the root cause? (code change, model change, prompt change, configuration change)
How many in-flight sessions were affected? Of these, how many were gracefully drained vs force-terminated?
Which runbook step took the longest? Was this expected?
Which runbook step failed or required deviation? What was the workaround?
What was the user-visible impact? (errors seen, sessions terminated, wrong outputs received)
What pre-deployment check would have caught this before it reached production?
Action items (owner, due date): _______________
Runbook improvements needed: _______________
The following are the fundamental principles for rollback runbooks in agentic AI services:

An AI service rollback is not a single action — it is a coordinated reversion of five artifact families: application code, model version, system prompt, feature flags, and tool configurations. Reverting only one without the others can produce worse outcomes than the original regression.

The session drain phase is unique to agentic services. Always handle in-flight sessions explicitly: either graceful drain with a time-box, or immediate termination with user notification. Never leave session state in an undefined intermediate state.
The pre-rollback snapshot is not optional. It provides the forensic evidence needed to understand what changed, enables a targeted rollback, and documents the state of the system for the post-mortem.

Every runbook step must include a verification assertion. Do not proceed to the next step until the previous one is confirmed. This is the discipline that prevents a rushed rollback from making the situation worse.

Runbooks rot. Schedule quarterly reviews and enforce runbook updates as part of the deployment pipeline. A runbook that has not been executed or reviewed in six months should be treated as suspect until verified.

The fastest mitigation is a feature flag disable. Always attempt this first before executing the full rollback. Reserve the full rollback for situations where the flag disable is insufficient or the regression spans infrastructure.

Post-mortems are investments in future rollback speed. Each incident that reveals a gap in the runbook is an opportunity to close that gap before the next incident. The goal is a runbook that gets faster and more reliable with each execution.
